medical_history.chief_complaint: abnormal behavior following immersion in warm bath water at home
medical_history.history_of_present_illness: At the age of 6 months, he had three episodes of abnormal behavior following immersion in warm bath water at home. He suddenly became unresponsive and hypertonic, his eyes gazed upward, then he became floppy. The whole attack lasted about 1 minute.
medical_history.past_medical_history: Psychomotor development was normal. The family history was negative for seizures.

physical_examination.general_examination: Neurologic examination, electroencephalographic (EEG) investigation, and electrocardiography (ECG) were normal. Vagal stimulation test by ocular compression was positive.
physical_examination.specialty_examination: EEG abnormalities started 25 seconds after placing the child into the bath; water temperature was 37°C. Five seconds later, the boy had shoulder jerks. He became unresponsive, stared, and had clonic movements of the lips. Consciousness returned 2 minutes later, with tears. He then fell asleep. There was no fever.

other_tests.findings: EEG abnormalities started 25 seconds after placing the child into the bath; water temperature was 37°C. Five seconds later, the boy had shoulder jerks. He became unresponsive, stared, and had clonic movements of the lips. Consciousness returned 2 minutes later, with tears. He then fell asleep. There was no fever.

other_tests.procedure_name: Vagal stimulation test by ocular compression

diagnosis.diagnostic_reasoning: The diagnosis was confirmed by video-EEG monitoring during bathing, which showed EEG abnormalities starting after placing the child into the bath. The absence of fever during seizures ruled out febrile convulsions, and the absence of bradycardia or other abnormalities on simultaneous ECG ruled out vagal fits. The absence of dermatographism and the presence of abnormalities on EEG during attacks ruled out aquagenic urticaria.
